title: NATO Summit Preparations Put Turkey at the Center of Transatlantic Fractures
subhead: Six days before hosting 32 NATO leaders, Ankara pushes for European defense inclusion, faces a deepening opposition crackdown, and navigates a $700M US arms sale bypassing Congress — all as the alliance's strategic direction tilts away from Europe.

sections.heading: NATO Summit: Gold Charts, Marble Palaces, and the Decoupling Question
sections.body:
Turkey hosts 32 NATO leaders on July 7-8 at Erdogan's presidential palace in Ankara, a summit designed to showcase alliance unity but overshadowed by accelerating US-European tensions. NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte previewed the pitch at the White House last week, holding up a chart emblazoned with 'THE TRUMP TRILLION' in gold lettering to demonstrate additional European defense spending since 2017. According to a NATO diplomat quoted by AFP, the summit is 'meant to be a demonstration to Trump of what a success he has had.'

But behind the pageantry, the CFR analysis warns that US decoupling from European security is accelerating. The Pentagon has announced cuts to NATO-assigned F-16 and F-15 jets by a third, strategic bombers by half, and the removal of approximately 5,000 troops from Germany. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called the refusal of some European allies to provide base access during the Iran war 'shameful' and announced a six-month review of US force posture in Europe. Secretary of State Marco Rubio called the summit 'probably the most important meeting in NATO's history' because there are 'some things here that need to be cleared up and fixed.'

Turkish Defence Minister Yasar Guler pushed back on the crisis framing, telling Reuters: 'We evaluate the period we are going through not as a crisis, but as a process of adjusting to the changing security environment.' He insisted the US has no intention of withdrawing from NATO, but wants European allies and Canada to assume more responsibility. The summit will be kept short — Trump flies in for dinner on July 7, with a single formal session the next day — limiting the chances for controversy, according to AFP.

Plans for a follow-on NATO summit in Albania next year have been thrown into doubt due to Trump administration resistance and dissatisfaction with Tirana's low defence spending, according to Reuters.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is positioning itself as the bridge between a decoupling US and a scrambling Europe — but the summit's success depends on keeping Trump content, not on resolving the underlying structural tensions pulling the alliance apart.

sections.heading: Turkey Demands European Defense Seat, Pushes for SAFE Program Access
sections.body:
Defence Minister Guler used his pre-summit Reuters interview to argue that excluding Turkey from European defense initiatives is a 'strategic mistake'. Erdogan echoed this on June 29, calling for Turkey's inclusion in all European security and defense structures while urging the removal of restrictions on defense trade among NATO allies.

At stake is access to the EU's €150 billion SAFE (Security Action for Europe) initiative. Under current rules, companies from non-EU countries — including Turkey, the UK, and the US — may contribute no more than 35% of component value in projects financed through the program. Turkish firms would need a security agreement with the EU, approved by all 27 member states, to gain wider participation. That is a high bar given ongoing tensions over human rights and rule of law.

Guler also confirmed Turkey's commitment to NATO's new defence spending targets, saying Ankara intends to gradually increase military expenditures to reach 5% of GDP by 2035 while fulfilling all agreed capability targets by 2029. Priority investments will include unmanned aerial vehicles, counter-drone technologies, missile and air defense systems, naval modernization, autonomous combat platforms, and cybersecurity.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is the second-largest military in NATO and sits at the alliance's most volatile frontier — yet it remains excluded from the EU's main defense financing mechanism. The 5% spending pledge is ambitious for an economy still running near-33% inflation, and the SAFE access question will test whether Europe's defense architecture can accommodate its most capable non-EU member.

sections.heading: $700M F-16 Engine Sale Bypasses Congress as Questions Mount
sections.body:
The Trump administration is pushing ahead with the sale of dozens of F-16 fighter jet engines to Turkey worth hundreds of millions of dollars, bypassing congressional objections, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters on June 24. The deal, valued at approximately $700 million, includes General Electric F110 engines — a critical component for Turkey's F-16 fleet.

Congressional questions are mounting ahead of the NATO summit. Critics note the sale sidesteps the normal notification process, raising concerns about executive overreach on arms transfers, particularly given Turkey's record of purchasing Russian S-400 systems and its crackdown on democratic institutions. The timing — just before Ankara hosts the summit — suggests the administration wants to demonstrate goodwill toward Erdogan, whose cooperation is seen as essential for a smooth summit.

The engine sale also intersects with Turkey's broader air defense recalibration. Defence Minister Guler confirmed Ankara is evaluating the purchase of both US-made Patriot missile systems and the Franco-Italian SAMP/T platform, according to Novinite. He said Turkey is 'open to any form of cooperation that meets our national security requirements, includes technology transfer, joint production, is sustainable, and reflects the spirit of the Alliance.' Turkey's national 'Steel Dome' integrated air defense system, unveiled in 2024, combines Turkish-produced missile systems, radars, electro-optical sensors, communications networks, and command centers into a unified shield.
sections.whyItMatters: The F110 engine sale is both a practical necessity for Turkey's aging F-16 fleet and a political signal: the US is willing to override Congress to keep Erdogan aligned ahead of the summit. The Patriot/SAMP-T evaluation signals Ankara is hedging its air defense bets while building domestic alternatives.

sections.heading: Erdogan Rejects Israel's Armenian Genocide Recognition, Turns Accusation Back on Gaza
sections.body:
Erdogan dismissed Israel's cabinet-approved proposal to recognize the mass killings of Armenians by the Ottoman Empire as genocide, calling it a slander by a 'criminal network which has the blood of 73,000 innocent people of Gaza, mostly children and women, on its hands.' He declared: 'Our history is free from genocide, massacres, oppression, and colonialism.'

The Israeli proposal, introduced by Foreign Minister Gideon Saar, still requires parliamentary approval. Saar noted that 32 countries, including the US, have already classified the violence as genocide and accused Turkey of running an 'institutionalized campaign of denial and minimization.' Armenia's Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan offered a cold response, suggesting the Israeli decision was politically motivated given the deterioration in Israel-Turkey ties.

The exchange marks a new low in Israel-Turkey relations. Israel had long avoided recognizing the Armenian genocide specifically to avoid angering Turkey, but that calculation shifted as bilateral ties deteriorated over Erdogan's outspoken criticism of Israeli policies toward Palestinians. Turkey's Foreign Ministry called the move a 'politically motivated' step designed to distract from Israel's actions in Gaza and proceedings at the International Court of Justice, according to the LA Times.
sections.whyItMatters: Israel weaponizing the Armenian genocide issue against Turkey is a diplomatic escalation that puts Yerevan in an uncomfortable position — acknowledging the recognition while rejecting the motive. For Erdogan, the genocide question remains a red line, but his counter-accusation linking it to Gaza deaths reveals how the Israel-Turkey rift has become structural, not situational.

sections.heading: Opposition Under Pressure: CHP Leader's Ouster, Imamoglu's Detention
sections.body:
Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) has been thrown into disarray after an Ankara appeals court annulled the 2023 party congress that elected Ozgur Ozel as leader, effectively removing him and reinstating former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu. Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu — widely seen as Erdogan's most formidable rival — was arrested in March 2025 on corruption and terror-related charges and faces a nearly 4,000-page indictment with 142 offenses carrying a combined potential sentence of up to 2,430 years in prison.

Ozel's team resigned from the CHP assembly on June 11 in a maneuver to trigger an extraordinary congress to re-elect him as chairman, Reuters reported. Rival leaders — the elected Ozel and the court-appointed Kilicdaroglu — held dueling meetings with party lawmakers on June 9, deepening the crisis. Ankara Mayor Mansur Yavas said the court decision was aimed at 'breaking' the CHP and rendering it ineffective.

The Jerusalem Post's analysis notes that these moves contribute to what some observers describe as a transition from 'competitive authoritarianism' to outright autocracy, where elections remain but the regime systematically removes viable opponents before they can contend for power. Erdogan's current bloc falls short of the 360 MPs needed to call a snap election that could reset term limits, but his nationalist ally Devlet Bahceli has been calling for a constitutional amendment to let Erdogan stand again in 2028.
sections.whyItMatters: The legal neutralization of Turkey's opposition is accelerating ahead of the NATO summit, with the CHP leader ousted by court order and the most popular challenger facing a multi-millennium prison sentence. Western leaders will dine at Erdogan's palace days after his main political rivals were removed from the field.

sections.heading: Economy Under Strain: Central Bank Holds at 37%, Inflation at 32.6%
sections.body:
Turkey's central bank left its key interest rate unchanged at 37% for a third consecutive meeting on June 11, monitoring the inflation impact of the Iran war. Turkish consumer price inflation came in at 1.7% monthly and 32.6% annually in May, slightly above expectations, according to Reuters — highlighting price fallout from the Iran conflict that disrupted energy supplies and raised shipping costs.

The Garanti BBVA CEO anticipates a rate cut in July, though likely a small one, according to MSN. But the central bank faces a difficult balancing act: the Iran war's energy shock has complicated what would otherwise be a disinflation trajectory, and the lira continues to lose ground. Turkey entered technical recession in 2025, with GDP contracting 0.2% for two consecutive quarters and annual inflation near 49% at that point. Erdogan's longstanding pressure on the central bank to keep rates low contributed to the lira losing over 80% of its value in five years.

The 5% of GDP defence spending target Guler announced for 2035 will require significant fiscal expansion on top of an already strained budget — raising questions about how Turkey plans to finance both its military ambitions and its economic recovery simultaneously.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's central bank is trapped between inflation that won't die and an economy that needs stimulus. The 37% policy rate is among the highest in the world, yet real rates are only barely positive. Adding a 5% GDP defence spending target on top of recession-level growth creates a fiscal credibility problem that the NATO summit pageantry won't solve.

sections.heading: Kurdish Insurgency Flares on Iran Border as New Group Emerges
sections.body:
A newly formed Kurdish group calling itself Khori Hiva (Sun of Hope) claimed responsibility for an attack that killed two IRGC members in the border town of Paveh in Iran's Kermanshah province on June 30, according to Al-Monitor. Two other IRGC members were injured, one of whom is in a coma. The group announced its establishment and said its goals include promoting Kurdish national identity and confronting the Iranian government's policies.

In a separate attack on the same day, an IRGC member and his wife were killed in Saravan in southeastern Sistan and Baluchistan province. No group claimed responsibility for that attack. The incidents are part of a series of escalating clashes: on June 27, two Iranian military personnel were killed and five wounded at a checkpoint in Baneh, and on May 30, Iranian counterterrorism forces conducted a raid in Kermanshah, killing two people identified as 'terrorists.' The IRGC has accused Kurdish groups of being backed by the US and Israel.

Iran has intensified its crackdown on Kurdish groups in the northwest following the US-Israel-Iran war that erupted on February 28 and ended with a memorandum of understanding on June 17. The IRGC dismantled a six-member team entering from the northwestern border, Sepah news reported. Also on June 30, IRGC Navy political deputy Mohammad Akbarzadeh — who had served as the Navy's spokesperson and made regular statements about the Strait of Hormuz during the war — died in a vehicle accident in Kerman province, according to Fars News. The EU had sanctioned Akbarzadeh on June 8 over his role in measures restricting navigation through Hormuz.
sections.whyItMatters: The emergence of Khori Hiva signals that the Iran war's aftermath is fueling a new phase of Kurdish militancy on Iran's border — a front that directly affects Turkey's security calculus. Turkey has its own unresolved Kurdish question, and any spillover from Iranian Kurdistan into Turkey's southeast would complicate Ankara's already dense security portfolio.

bottomLine: Six days before 32 NATO leaders land in Ankara, Turkey is navigating a stack of contradictions: it wants European defense inclusion while its opposition is being legally dismantled, it accepts a $700M US arms sale while evaluating European missile systems, it insists NATO is merely 'adjusting' while the alliance's strategic direction tilts sharply away from Europe, and it pledges 5% GDP defence spending while its economy runs 32.6% inflation and its central bank holds rates at 37%. The summit will likely produce a gilded communique and some defense contracts, but the underlying fracture — a US that is actively decoupling from European security, and a Turkey that wants to be at both tables — will not be papered over by gold-lettered charts.
sources: Reuters, Al-Monitor, CFR, Novinite, Malay Mail/AFP, LA Times, Politico, Jerusalem Post, Israel National News, Hengaw, Iran International, Fars News
